speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 3. January 6, 1997. Yvonne de Vries interviewing Stan Rubens. I want to go a little bit back to your hiding place. Did your parents pay money for hiding there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yeah. They, they paid money for a hiding place. And how much they paid I have no idea. But I think it was double the normal rent, something to that effect.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how-- where did they get the money from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my, my father had, had money because of the business. He had squirrelled money away, thrifty-like. And apparently, he had, he had money. So so far, so far that there would have come a point where he would be out of money. And he had to be, of course, very careful because he didn't know how long the war was going to last and if his money was going to last. And I think the attitude was, as long as I have the money and the people are willing to accept the money and cover me, until nobody's willing to cover me and I don't have the money, then all I can do is, OK, I'll go to the Germans and give up. Or I show myself or whatever. We were kind of phlegmatic about it by saying, OK, we will stick it out as long as possible. If we run out of money, OK, then I guess that's what's he'll, what we'll do.

speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been a, a tremendous strain for him. As a kid, you don't realize that. It must have been a tremendous amount of strain for him trying to figure out, how am I going to work all this? How, how will I make it through these war years, buy food? I have a daughter, a son, and, and, and ourselves-- my father and mother. How to do all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And again, this, this tension and, and this fear was, was continuously there. And this is what made it, made it so, I, I think, horrifying for, for everybody. Because we didn't know what to do. We couldn't get out of the country. We had tried to get out of the country when the war broke out and drove to [NON-ENGLISH], trying to see if we could board a vessel. We tried to board a vessel. Fortunately, we didn't board it because we found out later on it was torpedoed by the Germans. We didn't know-- in, in today's day and age, if I would have had to cross the border, I would figure out a way at my age to cross the border. But my father and mother were not of the type of people that-- hey, they were a sedate type people in their 40s, 45 years old or so. They were not very active. They-- climbing a mountain wouldn't cross their mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they kind of were stuck. They, they couldn't move. They're-- they were paralyzed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, this whole thing, to avoid being paralyzed, manifested itself for me that I became totally enchanted with sailing of boats. And I had my own boats with the idea in mind that, whatever happened in this world, I would have my own transportation, and I would be able to transport myself from point A to point B away from a bad area so that I would not be stuck like everybody else was during the war. I would be independent. And I executed that totally, that I would be totally independent. Then later on became old enough that I felt it wasn't necessary anymore because in this day and age, wherever you go, if there's a nuclear bomb, forget it. I would be wiped out, anyhow, wherever I would be. But anyhow, maybe I'm getting ahead of things here. But you may have some other questions on, on your mind. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I have another question. When the Germans came to your hiding place did they ask for papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think they asked for papers. They just said, well, we got you, Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't ask anything at all. Did your, did your family have false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had false, we had false papers, yeah. We did. And everything we had was pretty well taken away. We were told we could pack a few things, some toiletries maybe and some, some underwear and just a few things, I mean, in a small kit bag. And that's all we had. Later on, my father befriended in the Schouwburg-- in the theater-- a German fellow. And he was trying to figure out how to get away. So he befriended this German fellow and even got, got back to the hiding place with this man. He had taken one of my mother's diamond rings that she had put away. And he had told this German fellow, said, look, if you take me over to the hiding place, then I will-- because I don't know the exact reason why he wanted to get back. But he wanted to get back there because there was some stuff there that he wanted to get to. He said, I have in one of the, literally tucked away in the side of a easy chair, I have a diamond ring. And I'll give you the diamond ring if you'll take me over to the, to the hiding place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father went back to the hiding place and palmed the ring, and then went into the side of the easy chair, and said, here is the ring for you. And this same German fellow then agreed that-- Was it a German Jew or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. A German Christian person who was kind to my father. My father gave him money, and he gave him diamonds, but he was kind all the same. He said, look, he said, I tell you what I will do for you. When the truck comes for you to take you to the railway station, when you get in on the left-hand side at the end, I will untie the rope so that the flap is loose.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you get to there, before the car starts to move, there will only be guards on one side of the trucks. There will not be guards on the traffic side of the trucks. Jump off the truck as quickly as you can and disappear into the night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is what he did. But first I had to escape, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have another question, though. The-- what is called the Hollandsche Schouwburg, the Dutch theater--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: --could you explain to us what that place was about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I have good memories on that. All the seats were taken out. The stage was still there. And people were sleeping on the floor in all kinds of makeshift bedding. Everybody had, like, a little corner staked out for themselves. Well, this is my little corner with some, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some bedding, some pillows, and a little bit of luggage that we had. Everybody was using the limited amount of bathroom facilities. There was an entrance. You, you see, in a theater, they can control the, the in and exits very well. So it was a strategic, smart move to use this place to lock people up. Because you could lock the main doors, and nobody could get, could get out of the damn place. There was an outside little patio area with high walls. Yeah, nobody was going to climb that wall, because there was always German with, people with, German guys with, with rifles on their shoulder. I mean, you try to climb the wall, they'll shoot you down by two shakes of a lamb's tail. So it was a jail, in actuality. It was a jailed area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were your parents located?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I usually always found them. They were in a certain area. And there was a balcony also, and some people, I guess, were sleeping on the balcony. They were at a lower level. And other people were sleeping on the stage. And it was a noisy area. It was a smelly area.

speaker: interviewer
text: The place was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]. Everything was, what about this? What about that? What is going to happen to us? Question, question, question. How do we get out of here? Nobody got out of there. It was not possible to get out. How many people were there, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would imagine there were about 600 people there, 600, 700 people. It was crowded like hell. It was very crowded.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so how did you go across the street to visit your parents? How did this go? Were there German soldiers guiding you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There were German soldiers and the, the, the Jewish advisory board. People from that board would take us across at 2 o'clock. And on that particular day when my father knew that we were going to be on transport, we were told to take all our belongings from the creche and take them with us because we are going to stay with our parents. Then my father said, you haven't escaped yet, never telling me about how my sister had escaped. And I said, no. I said-- well, he said, I got to figure out a way for you to escape. He said, I tell you what you do. I'm going to tell one of the advisory board members to take you across the street because you left a sweater behind, and you may need a sweater because we're going to a cold climate. As you get halfway-- there was a street car, two-way traffic street cars on that road. And where the streetcar would stop right in front of the Schouwburg, there was an, an area for people to stand-- a safe, safe haven area. He said, the moment you get to the first safe haven area, when he takes you across the street he's not going to-- just walk normally with him. You take off, and you run like hell. And I looked at him and said, OK, like I'll just follow instructions. This man-- don't know who-- took me across the street, and I get to the safe haven area for pedestrians to stand. And I start running, over, around the corner where the zoo is. I just-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he stood there and was totally flabbergasted by me taking off, which was the idea. Unfortunately, a German Nazi, who was part of the, the guards there, saw me take off and took off from the Schouwburg to chase me. As I am going around the corner, running as fast as my legs could, could, could carry me, I hear the hobnailed boots behind me going click, click, click, click, click. And I hear somebody screaming, halt, halt, halt! Stop, stop! Halt, halt! And I hear those boots coming because he, he could walk, run faster than I could. And I could hear those boots getting closer and closer and closer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And through my head goes, oh, god, he's going to shoot me. Oh, god, I'm not making it. Oh, god, I'm in for it. What is he going to do to me? He's gaining on me. And fear got into my legs and paralyzed me.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, fear was the paralyzing thing that happened to so many people. They didn't know what to do. They didn't know how to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I literally collapsed on the sidewalk close to the, the next cross street. I collapsed on the sidewalk. He picked me up, literally in the scruff of my neck and said, komm mit mir, come with me, and took me with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father had been watching through the glass windows of-- to see if I had gotten away. And all of a sudden, he sees the German running after me and coming back with me. And I'm being deposited, strangely enough, back into the creche at the very entrance. There were, like, five steps up, a double door on both sides of the entrance area. After the double door were two settees, one on each side on a white marble floor. How I remember all these things, I don't know. But it's, like, engraved in my, in my, my mind. And I sat on this, this dark straight bench-- there's enough room for, for two people to sit-- and literally crying and totally shook up. And I must have sat there for about 45 minutes. And after about 45 minutes, a man came to me and he said, come with me. And yeah, I docilely, I very quietly came with the man, and said, OK. So I came with him. And he took me to that side door and the exit door where my sister had escaped. Apparently, my father had talked to somebody from the, the Jewish advisory board and said, please do something. Let my son escape or get him out of here. And so I was guided, in bright daylight-- it must have been about 4 o'clock in the afternoon, I think, by then. In bright daylight, I just quietly walked away from the creche area in the Schouwburg. But I was wearing my star. And so I had a sweater that I had put over my shoulder to cover the star because I figured, by now it is important that nobody realizes that I am Jewish or that anybody sees me wearing a Jewish star because I may get picked up again. And I am trying desperately to pluck that star off my, off my chest because it was sewn on. And unfortunately, my mother had done a hell of a job. And I had a rough time trying to get that, that star. And I keep on plucking away, plucking away under my sweater, trying to literally tear that star off my chest. Meanwhile, I'm walking. I'm walking away quietly because I figured I have to put on act that I'm not in a hurry. If I run, I'm going to draw attention to myself. So I'm just walking away quietly, and I started walking and walking. Finally, I get to a, to a bridge. And I figure, I've got to get rid of this star. And finally, I got that star-- finally-- off my, off my, off my, my clothing, and I kind of rubbed it in a little ball and rubbing it and rubbing it. And I just-- I figured, hey, I don't think anybody is going to pay attention to a kid that's going, that's throwing a stone in the water. It was, like, eh, kids throw stones in the water. And I said, hey, I'll just throw it in the water here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I, I threw the star into the water, and finally, I felt I could walk more easier. I think that happened somewhere in the Waterlooplein where that happened. I was a little bit lost, and I had to find my way back to the, what it it? Frans Vermeylenstraat, the extension of the Albert Krapstraat, and Frans Vermeylenstraat, and where the Thyssen family lived, and where I had, I know I had-- was a safe house. So I started walking and walking. And I, I kind of walked slow. I was sauntering like a kid that has nothing to do and is just killing time. And-- What did the city look like? Did you notice anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not busy on the streets. There was very little traffic. And I kind of kept to the walls of the houses. I tried to stay in the shade so nobody would notice me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you were close to Waterlooplein, [INAUDIBLE] Square. It's very close to the Jewish area.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but I was partially lost for a while. It took me a while to, to find my way from there to the, to the Frans Vermeylenstraat. So I, I went to the wrong direction for a while. And then I must have walked the entire length of the Lairessestraat. And then somehow, finally, I got-- yeah. I must've gone to-- finally, I must have gone to the Lairessestraat and Frederiksplein, and then down Frederiksplein on the, on the Oostende. I walked on the Oostende. Now I remember. I walked back along the Oostende because I didn't want to go to the Westende, because I was afraid that somebody might see me and recognize me there. And it was still daytime. So I walked from the Oostende. And the Oostende also leads into the Vermeylenstraat. Luckily, I have a, a good sense of direction. And because, as I said, for a while I was lost, but then I got to the Thyssen apartment and rang the bell and ran up. And my sister was there. And so I was reunited with my sister. Now my parents still had to escape. So true to what the German had said, my father and mother got into this truck with the canvas covering. And they-- my mother walked straight-- and my mother took the initiative here, I found out later. My mother walked straight away to the back of the car, pushed the covering back, and said, it's loose. And she said, let's go. And she just jumped out straight away, without hesitation. At the same instant that my mother jumped out of the car, a streetcar came, and my mother immediately jumped into the streetcar. And the streetcar, who-- the, the driver most likely saw-- immediately, like, on commando, immediately took off. And my mother was gone. Because in those days it was, hey, you do what you can. You save yourself. You cannot worry about somebody else. Do what you can. Save yourself was, was the motto. My father jumped out of the, the car, and he walked into the other direction away from the streetcar. And-- no, no. He walked towards Artis also, the same direction I had tried to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Artis is the zoo?

speaker: interviewee
text: The zoo. Right. And he walked into that direction. And that same evening, my father and mother also came to the, to the, to the safe house, and we kind of were reunited.

speaker: interviewer
text: What day was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the day. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The month, the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it must have been September or October.

speaker: interviewee
text: '42. No, '43 by then, I think it is. By then it is '43. Because we have been already in the hiding place for a while. So we got in the hiding place in '41-- must have been '42. I have to double check that on my, on my, on my notes. But anyhow, we-- we were reunited, but my grandmother, my aunt, my cousin, we were not able to get them out. They were sent to Westerbork. I just had word, very sadly, and it was extremely upsetting. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] My grandmother, aunt, and cousin arrived in Westerbork and were immediately sent to, to Auschwitz in cattle cars. And immediately upon their arrival in Auschwitz, they were sent to the gas chamber. And this information came to me from my cousin with whom I was underground in Utrecht, whose son went to Westerbork to ferret this out. Because that was his grandmother-- his from-- my aunt was his grandmother. And they were sent through. They never had a, a chance in-- anything at all. They were just picked up, sent to Westerbork, from Westerbork directly into Auschwitz, and killed off. Like, like nothing. Anyhow, we were united. And we stayed at the Thyssen house for a short while, because we had to move then. It wasn't safe. Because he was involved in the underground, I think. He was a very good man. Later on, we went to a place called [NON-ENGLISH]. Where that was, I don't remember the place. We got in one evening. I never saw the outside. I never got out. Only [DRINKS] a few months later, my father had found a place for my, for me. My sister went somewhere else. He found a place, which was the chauffeur of an uncle of mine that lived in Utrecht, who had chauffeured for my uncle. And they were willing to take me in. And my cousin by the name of Andre, that I just mentioned, he was already there. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: His name was Andre Rubens?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was a-- yeah, he was a Rubens. But because his mother had married, he was a van der Stam. His name was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Van der Stam is V-slash-D-- abbreviated-- Stam is S-T-A-M. And then so Andre was, was already there. The people where I went to, I even don't remember how I got there. All I remember at the place, [NON-ENGLISH], we had a lady that had us underground, and food was starting to get scarce. And every time when there was any food left, the first thing which she would say is, well, let's finish the food because tomorrow there may be a bomb or we may not, we may not ever have a chance to eat it. So she was always eating up whatever that was left on the table. In those days, all I had was short pants, and it was starting to get chilly. And I had continuously cold legs. [LAUGHS] Cold is one of the underlying factors that seemed to follow me through the entire war. It was cold, cold, cold. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyhow, I went to Utrecht to the, to the ex-chauffeur of my uncle. The names of the people was-- his name was Kees van Amerongen and Tanta Dien Amerongen, Tanta Dien-- Auntie Dean-- and Uncle Kees. My cousin, Andre, whose dad had a son, who was-- his name was Peter. And he was about three or four years old, I think, at that time. They had a very little apartment in a poor, a really poor area of Utrecht. But they had hearts of gold. And I know that Tanta Dien-- I love her dearly. She, she-- they were just beautiful. They're just, just marvelous people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I asked you before if you had false papers. Did you have a false name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I did have a false name. I was named Jan-- John.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Brouwer. My father's name was Hank Brouwer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that name for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brouwer was spelled B-R-O-U-W-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name van Amerongen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Van Amerongen. V, period. And Amerongen is spelled A-M-E-R-O-N-G-E-N. van Amerongen. And on Kees, K-E-E-S. And Dien was D-I-E-N, Tanta Dien. And-- yeah, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did, did you-- did people teach you while you were in hiding? Were you still following classes or--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no way of, of-- there was no teaching that took place in any way, shape at all. Except when I got to Tanta Kees and-- Kees and Tanta Dien. It seemed to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were Christian people. It seemed like, all of a sudden, things-- the fear seemed to quiet down a little bit. Things got easier for a while. We, we had, we had books to read. Tanta Dien would go to the library. I, I, I loved reading, especially books about history. Andre-- we got some English study books, and we started to study the English language, Andre and I. He was kind of like the teacher, like, turning the pages, then now we do this, now we do that, and I was following. But I guess we were teaching each other the English language. So we did English language. And we, we would really have a schedule. In the morning, after we had something to eat, first thing we would do, we'd go and start working on our English. Then in the afternoon, from cigar boxes-- little cigar boxes-- a knife and, and a brick, we constructed a castle. The castle was about this big. And we kind of created our own toy. And the, the castle had a bridge that could be raised. We had-- we took the hinges from the, from the, the cigar boxes, because they had hinges on them, and with that we could hinge the, the drawbridge. And for years that castle was kept by the van Amerongens. I, I, I-- they may still have it. And we created [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like a medieval castle. And we played with that. We had fun with that. And we put it-- we put those castles together. Maybe we made more than one castle. And during the warm days, they had a little balcony. And the balcony was like, oh, yay high. So I would get a, a big tub of water and splash myself with, with water to stay cool and, and lay on the balcony. But I could not raise myself above the railing of the balcony because then people could see me. So I made sure I stayed below the, the level that the balcony was. We-- Andre and I-- I was a little kid, so I didn't need that much space to sleep. And Andre was a teenager at that time. So between the two of us, we slept in a, in a bed that was actually a one-person big bed, but we slept there. The two of us, we slept there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were not allowed to go  out of the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Never left the house. I was just-- and, and I wouldn't even go near the window because the people across the street could see us. So the curtains were always drawn, and I would kind of peek-- I would peek through the window from time to time to see what was happening. But I would make sure that nobody could see me. As a matter of fact, when there-- there were times when, when I needed to go and use the bathroom, and they would be out. I would not flush the bathroom when they were not home because the people downstairs could hear, could hear the flushing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about walking?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would walk on, on, on, on socks. I wouldn't wear shoes. I always walked on socks. Because we didn't want-- if there was nobody there, if they left the house, then we went in a, what you might call, in a quiet routine. Like, hey, nobody can hear us. Nobody can see us. We didn't exist. We just plain [INAUDIBLE]-- we didn't exist. We weren't there. Until there was a raid. And unexpectedly, there was a raid. And they were going from house to house. And this was at 9 o'clock in the evening, and I had to get out of the house. Because if they would find me there, I had no papers. At that time, I had no papers. And so what they did, they were the last house in a, in a block. And there was-- at the end of the balcony was a stone, a little brick wall. So Uncle Kees pushed me up the wall so that on the top of the wall, I could step on the roof. And it must have been November or December because it was freezing cold. Maybe it was January. No, it must have been January or February. And I, I went up the roof, but I didn't know that was the end of the-- the wall where I was was the end of, of, of the building. And as I'm getting up, and I'm trying to grab, all of a sudden I see I was at, I'm at two stories. And I almost-- by, by a hair, I didn't fall down the two stories. I mean, I was scared out of my wits that, oh, my god, I'm going to fall here. But I hung on for dear life, managed to claw myself up the roof from that little precipice-- I think that's the best way to, to describe it-- climbed on the roof, and hid behind some pipes that were there. Germans got through, but by now it is dark. It is dark. I did this in the darkness. That's why it was so, so scary, because there were-- you couldn't use a flashlight because you would draw attention to yourself. So in, in pitch blackness, I'm stumbling around the roof and put myself flat on the roof so nobody would see me close to some, something that was a little bit-- there are always obstacles on the roof. So that's where I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyhow, I, I stayed there for, oh, maybe four or five hours and then-- waiting for a signal that, hey, things are OK. I can come back again. And eventually, Uncle Kees came, climbed up the precipice said, Jan! Jan! Where are you? Jan? And I gingerly put my head up and said, I'm here. It's OK. It's OK. The Germans are gone. So I got back in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, we heard about a raid that was taking place. And we tried to have Andre get out of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear about these things? How did you know that there were raids?

speaker: interviewee
text: The underground-- apparently, we got, we got word from the underground that would get the news out. They're planning a raid there and there and there. Because in order to plan the raid, they had to make preparations. And these preparations, got-- some people got the, got the wind of this, got the wind of this. And there was always people spying on what the Germans were doing. And the underground was pretty active in Holland, so we got word. Like, we got-- through the underground we got extra--
